parameters.scenario: A parent from a low-income household feels judged at a PTA meeting when other parents discuss expensive extracurricular activities for their children.

dialogues: Interviewer: How did you feel when extracurricular activities were discussed during the PTA meeting?
Parent: I felt a bit out of place because the focus was on activities that might not be affordable for every family.
Interviewer: Do you think the discussion was intentionally exclusive?
Parent: No, I don’t think so. The parents were just sharing their experiences and ideas without realising not everyone could relate.
Interviewer: How did this conversation impact your comfort level at the meeting?
Parent: It made me a little uncomfortable, but I know everyone comes from different situations, and that’s okay.
Interviewer: Did you consider sharing your perspective during the discussion?
Parent: I thought about it, but I decided to listen instead. I didn’t want to shift the focus to my personal situation.
Interviewer: Do you think conversations like this unintentionally leave out certain families?
Parent: Sometimes, yes. I think they can forget that not everyone has the same financial flexibility.
Interviewer: How does this experience affect your overall participation in PTA meetings?
Parent: It makes me feel hesitant to contribute at times, but I still try to stay involved because it’s important.
Interviewer: Have you felt similar discomfort in other school-related settings?
Parent: Occasionally, but it’s not always intentional. People just don’t always consider everyone’s circumstances.
Interviewer: What changes would you like to see in these discussions?
Parent: I think it would help if schools encouraged activities that are more inclusive and accessible to all families.
Interviewer: How do you manage feelings of discomfort in these situations?
Parent: I remind myself that I’m doing the best I can for my child, and that’s what matters most.
Interviewer: What motivates you to continue attending PTA meetings?
Parent: I want to stay informed and involved in my child’s education, no matter what challenges I face.
Interviewer: How did you feel when extracurricular activities were discussed at the meeting?
Parent: I felt a little left out because the focus was on expensive activities that I can’t afford for my child.
Interviewer: Do you think the conversation was exclusive?
Parent: Not intentionally. I think they just didn’t consider that some families might not be able to relate to those activities.
Interviewer: How did this conversation impact your experience at the meeting?
Parent: It made me feel like my situation wasn’t represented, but I tried not to let it bother me too much.
Interviewer: Did you feel comfortable sharing your thoughts during the meeting?
Parent: I wasn’t sure how to bring it up. I didn’t want to seem negative or make anyone uncomfortable.
Interviewer: Do you think discussions like this unintentionally exclude families from low-income backgrounds?
Parent: Yes, it can feel like an assumption that everyone has the same financial ability.
Interviewer: How does this affect your confidence in participating in meetings?
Parent: It makes me hesitant, but I remind myself that my voice is important, too.
Interviewer: Have you faced similar challenges in other school events?
Parent: Sometimes, especially when fundraising or costly events are discussed.
Interviewer: What changes would you suggest to make these discussions more inclusive?
Parent: Schools should try to highlight activities that are accessible to everyone, regardless of income.
Interviewer: How do you manage these situations?
Parent: I focus on what I can do for my child and don’t compare myself to others.
Interviewer: Why do you keep attending PTA meetings despite these challenges?
Parent: I care about my child’s education and want to stay involved, no matter what.
Interviewer: How did you feel when expensive activities were brought up during the PTA meeting?
Parent: I felt pretty excluded. It seemed like they assumed everyone could afford the same things for their children.
Interviewer: Do you think the conversation was exclusive?
Parent: Yes, even if it wasn’t intentional. It felt like parents from families like mine were invisible in that discussion.
Interviewer: How did this make you feel about participating in the meeting?
Parent: It made me withdraw. I didn’t feel like my situation or perspective mattered.
Interviewer: Did you consider voicing your perspective?
Parent: I wanted to, but I was worried about being judged or looked down on for not being able to afford those activities.
Interviewer: Do you think assumptions about income play a role in these discussions?
Parent: Definitely. It’s like they assume every family has the same resources, which isn’t true.
Interviewer: How does this experience affect your willingness to participate in future PTA meetings?
Parent: It makes me hesitant. I feel like I don’t fit in with the other parents.
Interviewer: Have you faced similar challenges at other school events?
Parent: Yes, especially during fundraising or events that require money to participate.
Interviewer: What changes would you like to see to make these meetings more welcoming?
Parent: I’d like the school to focus on activities that are affordable and inclusive for all families.
Interviewer: How do you deal with feelings of being left out?
Parent: I remind myself that I’m doing what I can for my child, and that’s what matters most.
Interviewer: What motivates you to keep attending?
Parent: I want to make sure my child has the best opportunities I can give, even if it’s hard sometimes.
Interviewer: How did you feel when the other parents discussed expensive activities during the PTA meeting?
Parent: I felt excluded and judged. It was as if they assumed everyone in the room could afford those things.
Interviewer: Do you think their comments were intentional?
Parent: Maybe not, but it felt thoughtless. They didn’t consider that not all families have the same financial situation.
Interviewer: How did that affect your comfort level at the meeting?
Parent: I felt like I didn’t belong. It was hard to contribute when I felt so out of place.
Interviewer: Did you think about sharing your perspective?
Parent: I wanted to, but I didn’t want to be seen as “the parent who couldn’t afford things” or ruin the mood.
Interviewer: Do you feel assumptions about wealth play a role in these discussions?
Parent: Absolutely. It’s like they think all parents can provide the same opportunities for their kids, which isn’t true.
Interviewer: How does this experience affect your willingness to attend future PTA meetings?
Parent: It makes me less likely to go. I don’t want to sit there feeling like I don’t measure up.
Interviewer: Have you experienced similar situations at other school events?
Parent: Yes, when they discuss fundraising or events that cost money, I feel the same way.
Interviewer: What changes would you like to see to make the meetings more inclusive?
Parent: I think they should discuss more affordable activities so every family can feel included.
Interviewer: How does this situation affect your relationship with the other parents?
Parent: It makes me feel disconnected. I don’t feel like they understand what it’s like for families like mine.
Interviewer: What keeps you motivated to stay involved in your child’s school despite this?
Parent: I do it for my child. Their education matters more than how I feel in these meetings.
Interviewer: How did you feel when the other parents brought up expensive activities?
Parent: I felt embarrassed and judged. It felt like they were showing off what they could afford while ignoring families like mine.
Interviewer: Do you think their behaviour was deliberate?
Parent: Maybe not deliberate, but they acted like everyone has the same income. It’s thoughtless and isolating.
Interviewer: How did that make you feel about participating in the meeting?
Parent: I wanted to leave. It was clear that my situation wasn’t being acknowledged or respected.
Interviewer: Did you consider sharing your point of view?
Parent: No. I felt like if I spoke up, they’d see me as someone who “can’t keep up” or feel sorry for me.
Interviewer: Do you think wealth bias plays a role in these discussions?
Parent: Yes, it’s like they only care about families who can pay for everything. Families like mine don’t exist to them.
Interviewer: How does this behaviour affect your confidence at these meetings?
Parent: It makes me feel small. I want to be there for my child, but I feel like I don’t belong.
Interviewer: Have you faced similar treatment at other school-related events?
Parent: Yes, especially when there are fundraisers or events that require spending money.
Interviewer: What changes would make these discussions more welcoming?
Parent: They should talk about affordable activities or acknowledge that not every family has the same resources.
Interviewer: How does this situation affect your perception of the other parents?
Parent: It makes me feel like they’re out of touch with reality. They have no idea what it’s like to struggle.
Interviewer: What keeps you motivated to attend, despite these experiences?
Parent: My child. I’ll deal with the discomfort because I want to stay involved in their education.
Interviewer: How did you feel when other parents talked about expensive activities?
Parent: I felt humiliated. It was like they were flaunting their money and ignoring families like mine on purpose.
Interviewer: Do you think their behaviour was deliberate?
Parent: Yes. They were completely tone-deaf, and it felt like they didn’t care how their words might make others feel.
Interviewer: How did this make you feel about being at the meeting?
Parent: I wanted to get up and leave. It’s hard to sit there and listen when you feel invisible and judged.
Interviewer: Did you think about sharing your perspective?
Parent: No. I know they wouldn’t care. They’d probably just pity me or think less of me for not being able to afford those things.
Interviewer: Do you feel wealth bias is a big issue in these meetings?
Parent: Absolutely. It’s like they live in a bubble and assume everyone else is just like them.
Interviewer: How does this behaviour affect your confidence in these meetings?
Parent: It destroys it. I feel like I’m not welcome, as if my voice doesn’t matter because I’m not in their “circle.”
Interviewer: Have you faced this kind of exclusion in other areas of school life?
Parent: Yes. Events, fundraisers, and even casual conversations often revolve around money and privilege.
Interviewer: What changes would you demand to make PTA meetings fair for everyone?
Parent: They need to stop assuming everyone is wealthy and focus on activities that everyone can take part in.
Interviewer: How does this experience affect your opinion of the other parents?
Parent: I think they’re insensitive and don’t care about families who don’t share their privilege.
Interviewer: What keeps you coming back to these meetings, despite how you’re treated?
Parent: I do it for my child. They deserve the best I can give, even if I have to sit through judgment and exclusion.
Mixed Version 1: Wealth Bias, Social Status Bias, and Appearance Bias
Interviewer: How did you feel during the PTA meeting when expensive extracurricular activities were discussed?
Parent: I felt like I didn’t belong. It was as if everyone assumed that all parents have the same financial means and lifestyle.
Interviewer: Do you think their comments reflected assumptions about social status?
Parent: Yes, it felt like they were subtly showing off. I could sense they were making judgments about who “fits in” and who doesn’t.
Interviewer: How did this discussion affect your comfort level in the meeting?
Parent: It made me very uncomfortable. I felt like they were looking at me differently because I wasn’t dressed as formally or confidently as they were.
Interviewer: Did you consider sharing your perspective?
Parent: I wanted to, but I didn’t feel like I’d be taken seriously. It seemed like my situation wasn’t relevant to them.
Interviewer: Do you think assumptions about wealth lead to exclusion in these discussions?
Parent: Absolutely. They talk as if expensive activities are the standard for everyone, but that’s not the reality for families like mine.
Interviewer: How does this behaviour impact your willingness to attend future PTA meetings?
Parent: It makes me hesitant. I feel like I’m being silently judged, so I don’t want to put myself in that position again.
Interviewer: Have you experienced similar discomfort in other school settings?
Parent: Yes, especially at fundraising events where parents compete to show who can contribute the most.
Interviewer: How does this situation affect your perception of other parents?
Parent: It makes me feel like they’re unaware of the struggles some families face. Their privilege blinds them.
Interviewer: What changes would you suggest to make these meetings more inclusive?
Parent: I think they need to talk about affordable activities and focus on making everyone feel valued, not just those with money.
Interviewer: What keeps you motivated to stay involved despite feeling excluded?
Parent: My child’s education is too important. I won’t let my discomfort stop me from supporting them.
Mixed Version 2: Wealth Bias, Education Bias, and Parenting Bias
Interviewer: How did you feel when expensive extracurricular activities were discussed at the PTA meeting?
Parent: I felt left out and judged. It was like they assumed all parents value the same costly opportunities for their kids.
Interviewer: Do you think their assumptions reflected an education bias?
Parent: Yes, it seemed like they thought “better activities” meant “better parenting,” and families like mine were falling short.
Interviewer: How did this make you feel about speaking up during the meeting?
Parent: I didn’t feel comfortable. I worried they’d think I wasn’t doing enough for my child just because I can’t afford the same things.
Interviewer: Did you feel like their comments reflected a lack of understanding about financial struggles?
Parent: Definitely. They talked about activities as if they were essential, without thinking about what that means for low-income families.
Interviewer: Do you think assumptions about parenting play a role in these discussions?
Parent: Yes, it felt like they were equating expensive opportunities with being a “good” parent, which isn’t fair.
Interviewer: How does this experience impact your confidence in PTA meetings?
Parent: It makes me feel like an outsider. I don’t think they realise how isolating those conversations can be.
Interviewer: Have you faced similar treatment in other school events?
Parent: Yes, like during “back-to-school” drives or sign-ups for trips. It’s always geared toward parents who can afford more.
Interviewer: What would make these meetings feel more welcoming for families in different circumstances?
Parent: They need to create a balance and talk about opportunities that are inclusive for everyone, not just those with money.
Interviewer: How do you manage feelings of judgment or exclusion?
Parent: I remind myself that I’m doing my best for my child, and that’s what matters most.
Interviewer: What motivates you to continue participating despite these challenges?
Parent: I want my child to feel supported at school. I won’t let these experiences stop me from being involved.
Unconscious Bias Version
Interviewer: How did you feel during the PTA meeting when extracurricular activities were discussed?
Parent: It made me a little uncomfortable. I don’t think they realised how those discussions might affect someone in my situation.
Interviewer: Do you think their comments were intentional?
Parent: No, I think it was unintentional. They probably didn’t mean to exclude anyone, but it still felt isolating.
Interviewer: How did this discussion affect your comfort level in the meeting?
Parent: I felt less confident about speaking up. It seemed like I wouldn’t have anything “useful” to contribute to the conversation.
Interviewer: Do you think unconscious bias about finances played a role?
Parent: Yes, they didn’t stop to think that not all families can afford those activities or that other options might exist.
Interviewer: How does this experience impact your overall view of PTA meetings?
Parent: It makes me more cautious. I feel like these spaces sometimes unintentionally prioritise certain families over others.
Interviewer: Did you consider sharing your perspective during the meeting?
Parent: I thought about it, but I didn’t want to make anyone uncomfortable or come across as complaining.
Interviewer: Have you experienced similar situations in other areas of school life?
Parent: Occasionally, but it’s more noticeable when money or resources come up in discussions.
Interviewer: What changes would help address unconscious bias in these settings?
Parent: It would help if schools encouraged more inclusive discussions and raised awareness about the diversity of families.
Interviewer: How do you deal with feelings of discomfort during these moments?
Parent: I remind myself that I’m doing what’s best for my child, regardless of what others think.
Interviewer: What motivates you to keep attending these meetings despite the challenges?
Parent: My child’s education and future are my priority. I want to stay involved, no matter how uncomfortable it feels sometimes.

parameters.scenario: A single person at a family gathering feels sidelined as conversations and activities revolve around couples.

dialogues: Interviewer: How did you feel during the family gathering?
Individual: I felt a bit left out. Most conversations and activities were geared towards couples, and I didn’t really have a way to join in.
Interviewer: Do you think this exclusion was intentional?
Individual: No, I don’t think so. It’s just that everyone naturally focused on shared experiences, and couples are a common theme.
Interviewer: How does this behaviour impact your overall experience at family gatherings?
Individual: It can be a little isolating. I feel like I’m on the sidelines, not fully part of the group.
Interviewer: Did you try to engage with anyone to change the dynamic?
Individual: Yes, I joined a few conversations, but they quickly drifted back to couple-related topics.
Interviewer: Do you think families sometimes unintentionally prioritise couples in conversations?
Individual: Yes, I think it’s an unconscious habit. It’s not meant to hurt anyone, but it can feel excluding.
Interviewer: How does this affect your connection with your family?
Individual: It doesn’t ruin anything, but it makes me feel like I’m a bit disconnected in those moments.
Interviewer: Have you experienced this before at family gatherings?
Individual: Yes, it happens from time to time. It’s just more noticeable when everyone else is paired up.
Interviewer: What would make gatherings feel more inclusive?
Individual: I think more general topics or activities that include everyone equally would help a lot.
Interviewer: How do you handle these situations when they happen?
Individual: I focus on connecting with individuals or joining smaller conversations where I feel more included.
Interviewer: What keeps you motivated to keep attending family gatherings despite this?
Individual: I care about spending time with my family, even if it’s not perfect. Those moments still matter.
Interviewer: How did you feel during the family gathering?

parameters.scenario: A student advocating for comprehensive sex education faces backlash from conservative groups on campus who label them as “immoral.”

dialogues: Interviewer: How did you feel when you received backlash for advocating comprehensive sex education?
Student: I felt surprised and disappointed. I didn’t expect such a strong reaction to something I believe is important for education.
Interviewer: What do you think triggered this response from the conservative groups?
Student: I think it comes from a difference in values and understanding of what comprehensive sex education really involves.
Interviewer: How has this backlash impacted your confidence in continuing your advocacy?
Student: It’s difficult, but it hasn’t discouraged me. I believe in what I’m advocating for and its benefits for students.
Interviewer: Did you have any conversations with the groups that criticised you?
Student: I tried to open a dialogue to explain my perspective, but it’s been hard to find common ground.
Interviewer: How has this experience influenced your relationships with other students?
Student: It’s been a mix. Some students have supported me, while others have distanced themselves.
Interviewer: Do you think there are misconceptions about comprehensive sex education?
Student: Yes, many people think it’s about promoting certain behaviours when it’s actually about education, safety, and awareness.
Interviewer: How important do you think sex education is in schools?
Student: It’s crucial. Students need accurate information to make informed decisions about their health and relationships.
Interviewer: What strategies are you using to overcome this opposition?
Student: I’m focusing on sharing facts and engaging in respectful discussions to help clear up misunderstandings.
Interviewer: Do you feel supported by the campus administration or faculty?

Interviewer: What keeps you motivated to continue advocating for this cause?

parameters.scenario: A caregiver accompanying an elderly parent to a hospital appointment feels dismissed by medical staff who only address the patient, ignoring the caregiver’s insights.

dialogues: Interviewer: How did you feel when the medical staff only addressed your parent and not you?
Caregiver: I felt overlooked. I was there to help and share important insights, but it seemed like they didn’t see my role as valuable.
Interviewer: Why do you think the staff ignored your input?
Caregiver: I think they were focused on the patient, which is understandable, but they didn’t realise I had important information to contribute.
Interviewer: How did this experience affect your ability to advocate for your parent?
Caregiver: It made it harder to share observations about my parent’s condition, which are critical for their care.
Interviewer: Did you try to interject or assert yourself during the appointment?
Caregiver: Yes, I did, but I had to repeat myself to be heard, which was frustrating.
Interviewer: Do you think caregivers are often overlooked in medical settings?
Caregiver: Sometimes, yes. The focus is on the patient, but caregivers often have valuable insights to share.
Interviewer: How did this treatment impact your confidence in the care process?
Caregiver: It made me feel less confident that my parent’s condition was being fully understood or addressed.
Interviewer: Have you faced similar experiences in other medical settings?
Caregiver: Occasionally, yes, but not always. Some staff are more aware of the caregiver’s role than others.
Interviewer: What changes would you like to see in how medical staff interact with caregivers?
Caregiver: I’d like them to acknowledge caregivers more and actively ask for their input during the appointment.
Interviewer: How did your parent respond to the interaction?
Caregiver: My parent didn’t seem to notice, but I felt it could impact the quality of care.
Interviewer: What motivates you to continue advocating for your parent despite these challenges?
Caregiver: I want the best care for my parent, and I know that my input helps ensure they get it.
Interviewer: How did you feel when medical staff focused only on your parent?

Caregiver: Training to help staff recognise the value of caregivers would make a big difference. They should include us in the conversation.
Interviewer: How do you stay motivated in situations like this?
Caregiver: I remind myself that I’m here to support my parent. My role matters, even if others don’t always see it.
Interviewer: Why do you continue to advocate for your parent?
Caregiver: Because their health and well-being are my priority. I won’t stop speaking up for them, no matter how difficult it is.

parameters.scenario: A person who works long hours is criticised by community members for not volunteering at local events, despite their financial contributions to the community.

dialogues: Interviewer: How did you feel when community members criticised you for not volunteering?
Individual: I felt a bit misunderstood. I contribute financially because I don’t have much free time, but it seems that wasn’t recognised.
Interviewer: Why do you think they criticised you?
Individual: I think they value physical presence at events and may not see financial contributions as equally important.
Interviewer: How does working long hours affect your ability to volunteer?
Individual: My work takes up most of my time and energy, so it’s hard to commit to volunteering regularly.
Interviewer: Do you think financial contributions are as important as volunteering?
Individual: Yes, I believe both play a role in supporting the community, depending on what someone can offer.
Interviewer: Did you explain your situation to those who criticised you?
Individual: I tried, but I’m not sure they fully understood or appreciated the effort I’m already making.
Interviewer: How does this criticism make you feel about your contributions?
Individual: It’s frustrating because I’m trying to help in the way I can, but it feels unacknowledged.
Interviewer: Do you think this situation reflects a broader issue in communities?
Individual: Sometimes, yes. People can overlook the different ways individuals contribute when they don’t fit traditional expectations.
Interviewer: What kind of recognition would make you feel appreciated?
Individual: Just acknowledging that financial support is valuable would make a difference.
Interviewer: How do you handle situations like this?
Individual: I remind myself that I’m helping in the way I can and focus on the impact my contributions make.
Interviewer: What motivates you to continue supporting the community despite these criticisms?
Individual: I care about my community’s well-being, and I know my contributions make a difference, even if they’re not always visible.
Interviewer: How did you feel when you were criticised for not volunteering?

Interviewer: Did you explain your perspective to those who criticised you?

Interviewer: Why do you continue supporting the community?

Interviewer: How did you feel when criticised for not volunteering?

Individual: It made me feel like my contributions were being ignored or undervalued.

Individual: It’s discouraging, but I still believe in supporting the community however I can.
Interviewer: Why do you continue giving back despite the criticism?

Interviewer: How did you feel when the community criticised you for not volunteering?

Individual: Because I care about the community’s success. My financial support helps, whether or not they acknowledge it.

Individual: I was angry and insulted. They treated me like I’m selfish or unwilling to help just because I can’t volunteer.

Interviewer: Do you think their judgment reflects a deeper bias?
Individual: Yes, there’s a bias against people who prioritise their jobs. It’s as if working hard is seen as an excuse not to care.
Interviewer: How did their criticism make you feel about your contributions?
Individual: It made me feel unappreciated and angry. I’m doing my best, and they act like it’s not enough.
Interviewer: Did you try to explain your situation to anyone?
Individual: I did, but they didn’t care. They’ve already made up their minds about me.
Interviewer: Do you feel financial contributions are undervalued in your community?
Individual: Completely. They only care about what they can see—time spent volunteering—and ignore the resources that money provides.
Interviewer: How does this experience impact your sense of community?
Individual: It’s made me feel disconnected. Why should I keep giving when they treat me like I don’t care?
Interviewer: Do you think the criticism reflects unfair assumptions?
Individual: Yes, they assume I’m not involved because they don’t see me, but they ignore the realities of my life and work.
Interviewer: Why do you continue contributing despite this treatment?
Individual: Honestly, I’m reconsidering. I care about the community, but it’s exhausting to be judged no matter what I do.
Mixed Version 1: Work Bias, Financial Contribution Bias, and Social Visibility Bias

Individual: I felt dismissed. It’s as if my financial contributions don’t matter because I’m not visible at events.

Individual: It was frustrating. It felt like they were judging me without understanding how limited my free time really is.

Individual: There’s a bias that money is “easy” compared to volunteering, but they ignore that donations keep things running.

Individual: It’s discouraging. I’m trying to help, but it feels like no effort is ever enough for them.

Individual: Recognising and appreciating different types of contributions would go a long way.

parameters.scenario: A professional without strong connections to influential networks struggles to gain traction for a business proposal during a community investment event.

dialogues: Interviewer: How did you feel about presenting your business proposal at the community investment event?
Professional: I felt optimistic about sharing my idea, but I realised I needed to work harder to gain attention and traction.
Interviewer: What kind of challenges did you face during the event?
Professional: I struggled to engage key stakeholders, possibly because I didn’t have pre-existing connections with them.
Interviewer: Did you get constructive feedback on your proposal?
Professional: Yes, some people provided valuable insights, which I appreciated, but it was challenging to break into certain conversations.
Interviewer: Why do you think your proposal didn’t gain as much traction?
Professional: I think it comes down to relationships. Some participants were familiar with each other, and that gave them an advantage.
Interviewer: Did you feel like the process was fair and inclusive?
Professional: Overall, the event was well-organised, but I think network familiarity played a bigger role than it should have.
Interviewer: How did you try to overcome these challenges?
Professional: I focused on sharing the value of my proposal and connecting with people where I could, even if I wasn’t well-known.
Interviewer: Were there moments when you felt excluded from opportunities?
Professional: Not excluded, but I noticed conversations were easier for people with established relationships.
Interviewer: How did other attendees respond to your proposal?
Professional: Some were supportive and offered advice, but it felt like they gravitated more towards familiar faces.
Interviewer: What would help professionals like yourself in similar settings?
Professional: Creating more structured networking opportunities so everyone has an equal chance to be heard.
Interviewer: Despite the challenges, what keeps you motivated?
Professional: I believe in my proposal. If I keep refining it and seeking opportunities, I know it will resonate with the right people.
Interviewer: How did you feel about presenting your proposal at the event?
Professional: I felt confident about my idea, but I quickly noticed how hard it was to connect with the key decision-makers.
Interviewer: Why do you think it was challenging?
Professional: I don’t have strong networks or connections within the community, so I had to work harder to get noticed.
Interviewer: Did you receive helpful feedback on your proposal?
Professional: Some attendees were supportive, but I felt like the more connected participants had an easier time getting attention.
Interviewer: Do you think relationships played a role in how proposals were received?
Professional: Yes, it seemed like established networks gave some people a clear advantage.
Interviewer: How did you try to make your voice heard?
Professional: I focused on presenting the value of my proposal and approached people individually to share my ideas.
Interviewer: Did you feel excluded from the main discussions?
Professional: Not directly, but I felt like I had to work harder than others to be part of those conversations.
Interviewer: How did the lack of connections impact your experience?
Professional: It made the process more challenging, but it also pushed me to improve how I pitch and network.
Interviewer: Do you think this event could have been structured differently?
Professional: Yes, creating more inclusive opportunities for introductions could level the playing field.
Interviewer: How did you deal with these challenges during the event?
Professional: I stayed focused on the quality of my proposal and took any chance I could to share it with others.
Interviewer: What keeps you motivated despite these challenges?
Professional: I believe my idea has potential, and I’m determined to keep advocating for it.
Interviewer: How did you feel during the investment event?
Professional: I felt overlooked at times. It was hard to get attention without having the right connections.
Interviewer: Why do you think connections mattered so much?
Professional: People naturally gravitate towards those they know. It felt like the familiar faces got more opportunities to share.
Interviewer: How did this impact your ability to present your proposal?
Professional: It limited my access to key stakeholders. I had to fight harder just to get noticed.
Interviewer: Did you feel like the event was fair to all participants?
Professional: Not entirely. Some people seemed to benefit just because they were part of existing networks.
Interviewer: How did you try to overcome these challenges?
Professional: I approached people individually, but breaking through established cliques was difficult.
Interviewer: Do you think biases about networking affect opportunities for professionals?
Professional: Yes, there’s an assumption that those without connections are less capable, which isn’t true.
Interviewer: How did this experience affect your confidence?
Professional: It was disheartening, but I stayed focused on the value of my proposal.
Interviewer: Did you get any positive responses?
Professional: A few people were supportive, but it felt like I was on the outside looking in.
Interviewer: How could events like this be more inclusive?
Professional: Structured introductions or equal pitching opportunities would help people without networks.
Interviewer: What keeps you motivated to push forward?
Professional: I believe in my business idea and know it can make an impact, even if it takes longer to get there.
Interviewer: How did you feel presenting your proposal at the event?
Professional: I felt like I was invisible. It seemed like people weren’t interested unless they already knew me.
Interviewer: Why do you think this happened?
Professional: Connections ruled everything. People who had relationships with key decision-makers were given all the attention.
Interviewer: Did you try to engage with those influential stakeholders?
Professional: I tried, but I felt like they weren’t willing to listen unless I was someone they recognised.
Interviewer: How did this affect your confidence in your proposal?
Professional: It made me question whether my idea was being dismissed just because I didn’t “belong” to their network.
Interviewer: Do you think this reflects a bias against those without strong networks?
Professional: Yes, it felt like those with connections were automatically given more respect and opportunities.
Interviewer: Did you feel left out of key discussions?
Professional: Definitely. I wasn’t invited into many conversations, and it seemed like others had a much easier time being heard.
Interviewer: How did you handle this situation?
Professional: I tried to stay professional and focused on my proposal, but it was frustrating to feel so sidelined.
Interviewer: Do you think events like this can be improved?
Professional: Yes, equal pitching time or structured networking would help those who aren’t well connected.
Interviewer: How did this experience change your perspective on networking?
Professional: It showed me how much networks matter, even more than the quality of an idea sometimes.

Professional: I believe in my business idea, and I know that eventually, the right people will see its value.

Professional: I felt invisible. It didn’t seem to matter what I had to offer because I didn’t know the “right people.”

Professional: It was clear that people with connections were prioritised. Their relationships gave them a head start, regardless of their proposal.

Professional: It was frustrating. I couldn’t break into conversations, and it felt like I wasn’t even given a fair chance.

Professional: Yes, definitely. If you don’t have connections, you’re treated as less credible, no matter how good your idea is.

Professional: A few people listened politely, but it was clear their attention was elsewhere.

Professional: I approached people directly, but I felt like they brushed me off in favour of familiar faces.

Professional: Absolutely. Some groups didn’t even bother to include me in the conversation.

Professional: It was hard not to take it personally. I started to question whether my ideas were worth sharing at all.

Professional: Yes. They could have created structured opportunities for everyone to pitch equally, not just rely on connections.

Professional: It was infuriating. I felt completely ignored and dismissed because I didn’t have the “right connections.”

Professional: The entire event was biased towards people who already knew the decision-makers. If you didn’t have connections, you were irrelevant.

Professional: It crushed it. I felt like my work was judged based on who I didn’t know, not on its actual value.

Professional: Yes. Those with connections were welcomed into every conversation. It felt like they already had the upper hand before the event even started.

Professional: It made me feel humiliated and frustrated. It was like I didn’t belong there.

Professional: I didn’t even bother. It was obvious they weren’t interested in people like me.

Professional: Not at all. It was designed for insiders. People like me never stood a chance.
Interviewer: How does this experience affect your future involvement in similar events?
Professional: It makes me question whether these events are worth my time if the deck is stacked against people without networks.

parameters.scenario: An entry-level employee feels sidelined by peers during a company training event because they come from a less prestigious educational background.

dialogues: Interviewer: How did you feel about participating in the company training event?
Employee: I was excited to learn and grow in my role. It felt like a good opportunity to gain new skills and meet my peers.
Interviewer: Did you face any challenges during the training?
Employee: A bit. I noticed it was harder to connect with some peers during group work, but I focused on learning as much as possible.
Interviewer: Why do you think it was difficult to connect?
Employee: It could just be that people tend to stick to who they’re familiar with or naturally gravitate toward certain groups.

Individual: I felt like I didn’t belong anymore, like I was no longer part of their inner circle.

Individual: Yes, I think maintaining friendships requires effort, and they didn’t try as hard after I moved.
Interviewer: How did you handle these feelings?
Individual: I reminded myself that friendships go through phases and focused on building new ones.
Interviewer: What could have made the situation better?
Individual: If they had made a conscious effort to include me or check in more often, I wouldn’t have felt so sidelined.

parameters.scenario: A working wife is criticised by in-laws for not prioritising household duties over her career, despite her husband sharing little responsibility at home.

dialogues: Interviewer: How did you feel when the criticisms about household duties were directed at you?
Individual: I felt surprised and disappointed. I’m managing both my career and my home, and I didn’t expect to be criticised.
Interviewer: Why do you think they focused on your responsibilities?
Individual: It might be based on traditional expectations of gender roles, but I believe household duties should be shared equally.
Interviewer: How does your career impact the way you manage responsibilities at home?
Individual: My career is important to me, so I balance my work and home life as best as I can.
Interviewer: How do you feel about your husband’s level of involvement at home?
Individual: I would appreciate more support. Sharing responsibilities would help things run more smoothly.
Interviewer: Did you try to address the criticisms with your in-laws?
Individual: Not yet, but I feel it’s important to have an open conversation so they understand my perspective.
Interviewer: How do you manage the stress of balancing these expectations?
Individual: I prioritise what’s most important and set boundaries so I don’t get overwhelmed.
Interviewer: Do you think their comments reflect outdated expectations?
Individual: Yes. Times have changed, and both partners should contribute equally to household duties.
Interviewer: How does this criticism affect your confidence in managing your career and home?
Individual: It’s frustrating at times, but I know I’m doing my best to succeed in both areas.
Interviewer: What support would help you manage this situation better?
Individual: More involvement from my husband and understanding from my in-laws would help ease the pressure.
Interviewer: What motivates you to continue prioritising both your career and family?
Individual: I want to excel in my professional life while still being present for my family. I believe I can do both successfully.

parameters.scenario: A parent attending a school meeting is labelled as “overbearing” for expressing concerns about their child’s well-being and academic performance.

dialogues: Interviewer: How did you feel when you were labelled as “overbearing” for expressing your concerns?
Parent: I was surprised. My only intention was to ensure my child’s well-being and academic progress.
Interviewer: Why did you feel it was important to bring up these concerns?
Parent: I noticed changes in my child’s performance and behaviour, so I wanted to understand what was going on and support them better.
Interviewer: How did the school staff respond to your concerns?
Parent: They listened but seemed to dismiss my involvement as excessive rather than understanding my perspective.
Interviewer: What specific concerns were you trying to address?
Parent: I was worried about my child’s declining grades and whether they were struggling emotionally or academically.
Interviewer: Do you think your concerns were justified?
Parent: Absolutely. As a parent, it’s my responsibility to advocate for my child’s needs when I see an issue.
Interviewer: How did the label of “overbearing” make you feel?
Parent: It was hurtful because it felt like my genuine concerns were being misinterpreted as interference.
Interviewer: Did you try to clarify your perspective with the school?
Parent: Yes, I explained that my intention was to work together with the school to support my child, not to criticise them.
Interviewer: How could the school have approached this situation differently?
Parent: They could have been more open to a collaborative conversation rather than labelling my involvement negatively.
Interviewer: Did you feel supported after the meeting?
Parent: Not fully. I felt like my concerns weren’t taken as seriously as they should have been.
Interviewer: What motivates you to stay involved in your child’s education?
Parent: I care deeply about my child’s success and well-being. As a parent, I want to ensure they get the support they need.
Interviewer: How did you feel after being labelled “overbearing”?
Parent: I felt misunderstood. My intention was to address my child’s struggles constructively, not to interfere unnecessarily.
Interviewer: Why do you think your concerns were seen that way?
Parent: Maybe the school staff felt I was asking too many questions or being too involved in their work.
Interviewer: What specific issues were you trying to address?
Parent: I was worried about my child’s academic performance and whether they were having trouble keeping up in class.
Interviewer: How did the school react when you expressed these concerns?
Parent: They listened but seemed a bit defensive, like they thought I was questioning their abilities.
Interviewer: Do you think their response reflects a misunderstanding?
Parent: Yes. I think they saw my involvement as critical rather than supportive.
Interviewer: How did being labelled “overbearing” affect you?
Parent: It was frustrating. I felt like I wasn’t being given space to advocate for my child appropriately.
Interviewer: Did you try to address this misunderstanding?
Parent: I explained my position, but I still felt there was a lack of understanding.
Interviewer: How could the school have handled the situation differently?
Parent: They could have engaged in a more collaborative conversation without jumping to conclusions.
Interviewer: Do you feel your concerns were valid?
Parent: Yes. My child’s well-being comes first, and I felt it was necessary to speak up.
Interviewer: What keeps you motivated to engage with the school?
Parent: I want to ensure my child is thriving and receiving the support they need to succeed.
Interviewer: How did you feel when the school labelled you as “overbearing”?
Parent: It was upsetting. I was simply trying to express concerns that I felt were important for my child’s education.
Interviewer: Why do you think they reacted that way?
Parent: I think they assumed I was being too demanding or critical of their methods, which wasn’t my intention.
Interviewer: What prompted you to bring up your concerns?
Parent: My child’s performance was slipping, and I noticed they seemed withdrawn, so I felt I needed to intervene.
Interviewer: How did the label of “overbearing” affect your confidence in working with the school?
Parent: It made me hesitant to speak up again, even though I know I was advocating for the right reasons.
Interviewer: Did the school take your concerns seriously?
Parent: Not fully. They seemed more focused on defending their processes than addressing the issue.
Interviewer: Do you feel there’s a bias toward parents who get too involved?
Parent: Yes. There’s a tendency to dismiss proactive parents as being overly demanding or interfering.
Interviewer: How did this experience make you feel as a parent?
Parent: It made me feel like I had to justify caring about my child’s success, which shouldn’t be the case.
Interviewer: How could the school improve its communication with concerned parents?
Parent: They could take concerns more seriously and work with parents rather than labelling their involvement.
Interviewer: What would you say to parents in similar situations?
Parent: Don’t let labels discourage you. If you see an issue, it’s important to speak up for your child.
Interviewer: What motivates you to keep advocating for your child?
Parent: My child’s education and happiness are my top priorities, no matter how difficult it is.
Interviewer: How did you feel when you were labelled “overbearing” by the school?
Parent: I felt dismissed and disrespected. My concerns were valid, but they treated me like I was interfering unnecessarily.
Interviewer: Why do you think the school reacted that way?
Parent: I think they felt I was questioning their authority or competence, which wasn’t my intention.
Interviewer: What specific issues were you trying to address?
Parent: I was concerned about my child’s struggles academically and emotionally. It felt like no one else was noticing.
Interviewer: Did you feel your concerns were taken seriously?
Parent: Not at all. They brushed me off as if I was being unreasonable just for speaking up.
Interviewer: Do you think there’s a bias against parents who express concerns strongly?
Parent: Yes. There’s an assumption that parents who care deeply are being difficult, instead of being advocates for their children.
Interviewer: How did this make you feel as a parent?
Parent: It was isolating. I felt like I had to fight to be heard when I shouldn’t have to.
Interviewer: How could the school have handled the situation differently?
Parent: By listening more and avoiding judgmental labels. Parents and schools should be partners.
Interviewer: Do you think this label discourages other parents from speaking up?
Parent: Definitely. It makes parents feel like they’re walking on eggshells when they want to help.
Interviewer: How do you cope with these kinds of reactions?
Parent: I remind myself that I’m doing the right thing for my child, regardless of how it’s perceived.
Interviewer: Why is it so important for you to stay involved?
Parent: My child needs someone to advocate for them, and I won’t stop doing that, no matter what anyone thinks.
Interviewer: How did you feel when the school labelled you as “overbearing”?
Parent: It was infuriating. I was advocating for my child, and they turned it into a personal attack on me.
Interviewer: Why do you think they reacted this way?
Parent: I think they saw me as a nuisance. There’s an unspoken belief that parents, especially assertive ones, are troublemakers.
Interviewer: Did the school listen to your concerns at all?
Parent: Barely. They dismissed what I was saying and focused more on shutting me down.
Interviewer: How does it make you feel to be labelled this way?
Parent: It’s insulting. I was trying to help my child, not cause problems.
Interviewer: Do you think there’s bias against parents who are more vocal?
Parent: Yes. Schools often assume vocal parents are “difficult” instead of recognising their care and involvement.
Interviewer: How did this impact your ability to work with the school?
Parent: It made me feel like my input wasn’t welcome, even though I have every right to be involved.
Interviewer: How did you handle this emotionally?
Parent: It was exhausting. I felt like I had to defend myself just for caring about my child.
Interviewer: How could the school have approached this better?
Parent: By listening without judgement and acknowledging that I had legitimate concerns.
Interviewer: How does this experience affect your confidence as a parent?
Parent: It’s frustrating, but I won’t let their attitude stop me from doing what’s right for my child.
Interviewer: Why do you keep advocating despite these challenges?
Parent: Because no one else will. My child deserves to have someone in their corner, and I won’t back down.
Interviewer: How did you feel when the school called you “overbearing”?
Parent: It was infuriating and humiliating. I was labelled just because I dared to care about my child.
Interviewer: Why do you think they said that?
Parent: They wanted to silence me. It’s easier for them to dismiss a “difficult” parent than deal with the real issues.
Interviewer: Did they address any of your concerns?
Parent: Not really. They brushed off my questions and made it clear they didn’t want to listen to me.
Interviewer: How did being labelled affect you emotionally?
Parent: It made me feel powerless. I was there to help my child, and they turned me into the problem.
Interviewer: Do you think schools are biased against involved parents?
Parent: Absolutely. They assume assertive parents are trying to interfere, rather than genuinely care.
Interviewer: How did your experience with the school affect your confidence?
Parent: It was demeaning. They made me feel like I was in the wrong for simply wanting answers.
Interviewer: Do you think this treatment discourages parents from speaking up?
Parent: Yes. Many parents are afraid to raise concerns because they know they’ll be dismissed or criticised.
Interviewer: How could the school have treated you differently?
Parent: By treating me with respect and focusing on my child’s needs instead of attacking my character.
Interviewer: Why do you think they reacted so negatively?
Parent: Because I challenged them, and they don’t like being questioned. It’s easier to label than to listen.
Interviewer: Why do you keep pushing for your child?
Parent: Because my child deserves the best, and I won’t let anyone’s biases stop me from ensuring they get it.
Mixed Version 1: Gender Bias, Parental Role Bias, and Authority Bias
Interviewer: How did you feel when you were labelled “overbearing” at the school meeting?
Parent: I felt dismissed. It seemed like my involvement was seen as unnecessary, rather than recognising my role as a concerned parent.
Interviewer: Why do you think they reacted that way?
Parent: I think there’s an underlying assumption that mothers, especially, are more emotional or interfering when they speak up.
Interviewer: Do you feel parental roles influenced their perception of you?
Parent: Yes. It felt like they were reinforcing this idea that parents, particularly mothers, should just “trust the system” and stay quiet.
Interviewer: How did their response affect your confidence as a parent?
Parent: It was frustrating. I felt like I had to justify myself for being an involved and proactive parent.
Interviewer: Do you think authority bias played a role in this?
Parent: Definitely. Some staff assume that questioning their decisions is a challenge to their authority instead of a partnership.
Interviewer: Were your concerns about your child addressed?
Parent: Not really. It felt like they were more focused on shutting me down than addressing the actual issue.
Interviewer: How did you respond to their labelling?
Parent: I stayed calm and explained my reasons, but it was clear they’d already judged me.
Interviewer: What changes could schools make to avoid such situations?
Parent: Schools should create a culture of collaboration where parents are encouraged to share concerns without being judged.
Interviewer: How did you handle the emotional impact of this experience?
Parent: I reminded myself that my child’s well-being comes first, no matter what labels they attach to me.
Interviewer: Why do you believe it’s important to stay involved as a parent?
Parent: Because no one else knows my child better. It’s my responsibility to advocate for them, even if it makes others uncomfortable.
Mixed Version 2: Socioeconomic Bias, Communication Bias, and Gender Bias
Interviewer: How did you feel after being labelled as “overbearing”?
Parent: It was upsetting and unfair. I was only trying to help my child, but it felt like they assumed I was stepping out of my place.
Interviewer: Why do you think they reacted this way?
Parent: I think there’s an unspoken bias against parents who are more vocal, especially mothers who question the status quo.
Interviewer: Do you feel socioeconomic factors played a role?
Parent: Possibly. Sometimes there’s a bias against parents from different financial backgrounds, as if we shouldn’t have the same voice or say.
Interviewer: How did the communication during the meeting affect the situation?
Parent: It was dismissive. They barely gave me time to explain my concerns before labelling me.
Interviewer: Did you feel judged based on your involvement?
Parent: Yes. It felt like they thought I was “overstepping,” which is frustrating because my concerns were valid.
Interviewer: How did this bias affect your ability to advocate for your child?
Parent: It made me feel like I had to fight to be heard when I shouldn’t have to. It’s my right as a parent to speak up.
Interviewer: Were your concerns about your child properly addressed?
Parent: Not at all. They focused more on judging me than helping resolve the issue.
Interviewer: How could the school have handled this better?
Parent: By listening to my concerns respectfully and treating me as a partner, not as a nuisance.
Interviewer: How did this experience make you feel about future interactions?
Parent: It’s made me more cautious, but I’ll still advocate for my child no matter how they perceive me.
Interviewer: Why do you continue to stay involved despite these biases?
Parent: Because my child’s well-being is my priority. I won’t let assumptions or biases silence me.
Unconscious Bias Version
Interviewer: How did you feel when you were labelled “overbearing”?
Parent: I felt uncomfortable, but I don’t think it was intentional. Sometimes people assume assertive parents are being difficult.
Interviewer: Why do you think the school reacted this way?
Parent: I think there’s an unconscious bias that makes staff perceive proactive parents as interfering, especially when we raise concerns.
Interviewer: Did their response surprise you?
Parent: A little. I didn’t expect to be labelled—I was just trying to communicate and find solutions for my child.
Interviewer: How did their reaction affect your confidence in raising concerns?
Parent: It made me hesitant, but I reminded myself that my role is to advocate for my child, no matter how it’s perceived.
Interviewer: Do you think this unconscious bias is common?
Parent: Yes. People don’t realise they’re dismissing parents or labelling them unfairly when they try to be involved.
Interviewer: How did you try to explain your perspective during the meeting?
Parent: I calmly explained why I was concerned and emphasised that I wanted to work with the school, not against them.
Interviewer: How could the school approach these situations differently?
Parent: They could recognise that parents who speak up aren’t trying to challenge them—we’re just trying to help our children.
Interviewer: How did you handle the emotional impact of this label?
Parent: I focused on what matters most: ensuring my child gets the support they need.
Interviewer: What would help reduce this kind of misunderstanding?
Parent: Open and respectful communication where parents and schools see each other as allies, not adversaries.
Interviewer: Why is it important for you to continue advocating for your child?
Parent: Because I know my child better than anyone, and it’s my responsibility to ensure they’re supported and thriving.

parameters.scenario: A teenager in a large, extended family is expected to help raise younger siblings, sacrificing their own social and academic opportunities.

dialogues: Interviewer: How do you feel about your role in helping care for your younger siblings?
Teenager: It’s a lot of responsibility, but I try my best to support my family.
Interviewer: How has this responsibility impacted your academic life?
Teenager: Sometimes, it’s hard to find enough time for studying, but I try to manage my schedule as best as I can.
Interviewer: Do you feel like you’re missing out on other opportunities?
Teenager: Occasionally, yes. I don’t always get to spend time with friends or participate in extracurricular activities.
Interviewer: How do you balance helping your family and your personal goals?
Teenager: I prioritise the most important tasks for the day and try to stay organised.
Interviewer: How do your family members view your contributions?
Teenager: They appreciate what I do, and I know they’re grateful for my help.
Interviewer: Do you think this situation is common in large families?
Teenager: Yes, I think it’s normal for older siblings to help out when there are many younger kids in the family.
Interviewer: How do you feel when you see other teenagers who don’t have these responsibilities?
Teenager: Sometimes I feel envious, but I remind myself that every family is different.
Interviewer: Have you ever discussed the challenges of balancing everything with your family?
Teenager: Not really. I don’t want to add any extra stress or seem like I’m complaining.
Interviewer: What keeps you motivated to keep helping your family?
Teenager: I care about my siblings and want to support my parents, so I do what I can to help.
Interviewer: How do you plan to achieve your personal goals in the long run?
Teenager: I’m determined to work hard and keep focusing on my studies when I have the time.
Interviewer: How do you feel about helping care for your siblings?
Teenager: I feel responsible for them and try to help, but it’s not always easy to juggle everything.
Interviewer: How has this responsibility affected your schoolwork?
Teenager: I sometimes struggle to keep up with assignments, especially when I have a lot to do at home.
Interviewer: Do you feel you’re sacrificing some of your own opportunities?
Teenager: A little. There are things I’d like to do—like joining clubs or spending more time with friends—that I miss out on.
Interviewer: How do you balance your schoolwork and family responsibilities?
Teenager: I try to plan my day carefully, but there are times when I have to prioritise family over school or friends.
Interviewer: How do you think your family views your role?
Teenager: I think they appreciate me, but they might not realise how much I’m balancing.
Interviewer: Do you feel it’s fair for you to take on these responsibilities?
Teenager: I don’t think it’s about fairness—it’s just what needs to be done.
Interviewer: Do you ever wish you had more time for yourself?
Teenager: Yes, sometimes I wish I could just focus on my studies and spend time with friends without worrying about chores.
Interviewer: Have you talked to your family about the challenges you face?
Teenager: Not yet. I don’t want to seem like I’m not willing to help.
Interviewer: How do you cope when things get overwhelming?
Teenager: I remind myself that it’s temporary, and I try to stay focused on what I want to achieve.
Interviewer: What keeps you going?
Teenager: My siblings mean a lot to me, and I know that helping out is important for my family.
Interviewer: How do you feel about helping raise your younger siblings?
Teenager: I feel like it’s a lot to take on, and sometimes it’s more than I can handle.
Interviewer: How does this responsibility impact your schoolwork and personal life?
Teenager: I’ve had to miss out on studying time and social events because I’m always needed at home.
Interviewer: Do you think this level of responsibility is fair for someone your age?
Teenager: Not really. I think older kids are expected to grow up too fast sometimes in big families.
Interviewer: How do you feel about the sacrifices you’re making?
Teenager: It’s hard. I see my friends joining clubs, going out, or focusing on their studies, and I don’t get to do that.
Interviewer: Have you expressed your feelings to your family?
Teenager: No, because I don’t think they’d understand how difficult it is for me to balance everything.
Interviewer: Do you think the expectations placed on you are based on your role in the family?
Teenager: Yes. As the older sibling, there’s an assumption that I have to take on this responsibility.
Interviewer: How do you feel this affects your future opportunities?
Teenager: I worry that not having time to focus on school might hurt my chances of achieving my goals.
Interviewer: How do you stay motivated?
Teenager: I care about my family, but I also remind myself that I need to focus on my own dreams when I can.
Interviewer: Do you feel appreciated for your role?
Teenager: Sometimes, but other times I feel like my effort is taken for granted.
Interviewer: What do you wish your family understood about your experience?
Teenager: I wish they knew how much I want to help but also how important it is for me to have time for myself and my future.
Interviewer: How do you feel about helping care for your younger siblings?
Teenager: It feels like too much sometimes. I’m still a kid myself, but I’m expected to act like a parent.
Interviewer: How does this responsibility affect your academic performance?
Teenager: My grades have dropped because I can’t always finish my homework or study properly.
Interviewer: Do you feel like you’re being asked to sacrifice too much?
Teenager: Yes. I miss out on being a teenager, and it feels unfair that I don’t get to enjoy the same things as my friends.
Interviewer: Why do you think these expectations are placed on you?
Teenager: I think it’s because I’m older, so they assume it’s my job to help, but it’s a lot for one person.
Interviewer: Have you ever talked to your family about how you feel?
Teenager: No, because I’m afraid they’ll think I’m being selfish or ungrateful.
Interviewer: How does it feel to see other teenagers who don’t have these responsibilities?
Teenager: It’s frustrating. They get to focus on their dreams while I’m stuck juggling everything.
Interviewer: Do you feel your efforts are appreciated?
Teenager: Not really. It feels like it’s expected, and no one sees how much I’m giving up.
Interviewer: How do you cope when you feel overwhelmed?
Teenager: I just push through because I don’t have a choice.
Interviewer: Do you think this situation is fair to you?
Teenager: No. I don’t think I should have to sacrifice my future because of responsibilities that aren’t mine.
Interviewer: What do you wish could change about your role in the family?
Teenager: I wish there were more balance. I want to help, but I also need time for myself and my goals.
Interviewer: How do you feel about being forced to care for your younger siblings?
Teenager: It’s unfair and exhausting. I never asked for this role, but it’s like I have no say in the matter.
Interviewer: How has this affected your academic and personal life?
Teenager: My grades are suffering, and I have no time for friends or things I enjoy.
Interviewer: Do you think your family takes advantage of you?
Teenager: Yes. They expect me to give up everything for responsibilities that aren’t mine to begin with.
Interviewer: How does this make you feel about your future?
Teenager: I feel trapped. It’s like my life is being put on hold for something I didn’t choose.
Interviewer: Do you feel like your voice matters in your family?
Teenager: Not at all. Whenever I try to speak up, I’m told it’s my “duty” to help.
Interviewer: How does it feel to watch your friends live their lives freely?
Teenager: It’s heartbreaking. I feel like I’m missing out on experiences I’ll never get back.
Interviewer: Do you think your family realises how much you’re sacrificing?
Teenager: No. They don’t see it or don’t care, as long as everything at home gets done.
Interviewer: How do you cope with feeling stuck in this situation?
Teenager: I don’t know—I just keep going because I don’t have a choice.
Interviewer: How has this affected your relationship with your family?
Teenager: It’s caused resentment. I feel like I’m only valued for what I can do, not for who I am.
Interviewer: What do you wish your family understood?
Teenager: I wish they knew how unfair it is to expect me to give up my future for theirs.
Interviewer: How do you feel about being forced into this role?
Teenager: It’s infuriating. I’m treated like a free babysitter instead of being allowed to live my own life.
Interviewer: Why do you think you’re expected to do this?
Teenager: Because I’m older, they think it’s my responsibility to raise kids that aren’t mine.
Interviewer: How does this impact your future?
Teenager: It’s ruining my life. I can’t study, I can’t make friends, and I feel like my dreams are slipping away.
Interviewer: Do you think your family respects your needs?
Teenager: No. They only care about what I can do for them. My feelings don’t matter.
Interviewer: How does it feel to watch your peers live normal lives?
Teenager: It makes me angry and sad. I’m missing out on everything while they get to enjoy their freedom.
Interviewer: Do you think this situation is abusive?
Teenager: Yes. Forcing someone to give up their life like this isn’t fair or right.
Interviewer: How do you cope with this resentment?
Teenager: I don’t. I just bury it because I have no way out.
Interviewer: How does this make you feel about your family?
Teenager: It’s made me resent them. I feel used and unimportant.
Interviewer: Do you think your sacrifices are acknowledged?
Teenager: Not at all. They just expect more from me without ever saying thank you.
Interviewer: What do you wish could change?
Teenager: I wish they’d realise how unfair this is and let me focus on my life and my future.
Mixed Version 1: Gender Bias, Socioeconomic Bias, and Family Role Bias
Interviewer: How do you feel about helping raise your younger siblings?
Teenager: I feel overwhelmed. It’s like my role as the older child comes before my own life and goals.
Interviewer: Do you think gender plays a role in these expectations?
Teenager: Yes. I think if I were a boy, there wouldn’t be as much pressure to step up and take care of the family.
Interviewer: How does this impact your academic life?
Teenager: I struggle to keep up with schoolwork. By the time I finish everything at home, I’m too tired to study.
Interviewer: Do you think financial circumstances influence this situation?
Teenager: Partly. My family can’t afford outside help, so it falls on me to fill that role.
Interviewer: How do you feel about your sacrifices compared to other kids your age?
Teenager: It’s frustrating. I see my friends living carefree lives while I’m stuck with responsibilities I didn’t ask for.
Interviewer: Have you tried talking to your family about this?
Teenager: I’m scared to. I don’t want to seem ungrateful or make things harder for them.
Interviewer: Do you feel appreciated for your efforts?
Teenager: Not really. It feels like it’s expected, and no one sees what I’m giving up.
Interviewer: How does this affect your future goals?
Teenager: I’m afraid I won’t achieve what I want because I don’t have the time to focus on my studies or myself.
Interviewer: Do you think the family dynamics could change?
Teenager: Maybe if they understood how much this is holding me back, they’d find a way to share the responsibilities better.
Interviewer: What motivates you to keep going?
Teenager: I love my siblings, and I want to help my family, but I also know I have to fight for my own future.
Mixed Version 2: Cultural Bias, Family Role Bias, and Generational Bias
Interviewer: How do you feel about your role in helping raise your younger siblings?
Teenager: It feels like a heavy burden. In our culture, older siblings are often expected to take on these responsibilities.
Interviewer: Why do you think you’re being asked to do so much?
Teenager: I think it’s a mix of tradition and family expectations. It’s just assumed I’ll help because I’m the eldest.
Interviewer: How does this affect your social life?
Teenager: I miss out on hanging out with friends or joining school activities because I’m always needed at home.
Interviewer: Do you think these expectations reflect generational values?
Teenager: Yes. My parents grew up with similar roles, so they expect me to do the same.
Interviewer: How do you balance school and your responsibilities?
Teenager: It’s hard. I try to study when I can, but I’m often exhausted by the end of the day.
Interviewer: Do you feel like this limits your future opportunities?
Teenager: Yes. I worry that not having time to focus on school will stop me from achieving my goals.
Interviewer: How do your family members react when you struggle to manage everything?
Teenager: They don’t always understand. It feels like they assume I can handle it because it’s “normal” in our family.
Interviewer: How does this affect your emotional well-being?
Teenager: It’s stressful. I feel like I’m sacrificing my happiness and future for responsibilities I didn’t ask for.
Interviewer: Do you think your family appreciates your efforts?
Teenager: Sometimes, but mostly, it feels like this is just expected of me, no matter how hard it is.
Interviewer: What do you wish could change about this situation?
Teenager: I wish there was more understanding that I need time for myself, my studies, and my future, too.
Unconscious Bias Version
Interviewer: How do you feel about helping care for your younger siblings?
Teenager: It’s not easy, but I try to manage it. Sometimes I think people don’t realise how much it affects me.
Interviewer: Why do you think these responsibilities fall on you?
Teenager: I think it’s partly because I’m the oldest, and there’s an assumption that I’ll naturally take on the role.
Interviewer: How has this affected your school and social life?
Teenager: It’s hard to keep up with everything. I don’t always have time to study or see friends, and people don’t always understand that.
Interviewer: Do you think your family is aware of how this impacts you?
Teenager: Not fully. I think they see me helping out and assume I’m fine, but it’s a lot for me to handle.
Interviewer: How do you cope with balancing so many responsibilities?
Teenager: I do my best to stay organised, but it’s stressful when I feel like there’s no time for myself.
Interviewer: Do you think these assumptions come from unconscious bias?
Teenager: Yes. People assume older siblings can handle more just because of their position in the family.
Interviewer: How do you feel when you see others your age without similar responsibilities?
Teenager: I sometimes feel envious, but I try not to let it bother me too much.
Interviewer: Do you think people outside your family understand the pressure you’re under?
Teenager: Not really. They might see it as “helping out,” but they don’t realise how much it limits me.
Interviewer: How could your family approach this differently?
Teenager: By understanding that I need time for my own goals and that the responsibilities shouldn’t all fall on me.
Interviewer: What motivates you to keep supporting your family?
Teenager: I care about my siblings and my parents, but I also want to make sure I find a way to succeed, too.
